title: This 275-Million-Year-Old Animal Had a Jaw So Twisted It Stumped Scientists for Years
summary: Have you ever run your tongue over your bottom teeth? They point straight up, right? Now imagine if they pointed sideways—out toward your cheeks! That's what scientists found in a 275-million-year-old animal called Tanyka amnicola. Its jaw was so twisted that the teeth stuck out to the sides, and the inside of the jaw was covered in tiny bumps like a cheese grater. Researchers dug up nine of these strange jaws in a dry riverbed in Brazil. At first, they thought the twist was a mistake—maybe the bones got squished over millions of years. But every single jaw had the same twist! "It's not a deformation, it's just the way the animal was made," says scientist Jason Pardo. Tanyka was a tetrapod (a four-legged animal with a backbone), like frogs, lizards, and even you! But it belonged to a really old group that mostly died out. Think of it like a platypus—a mammal that still lays eggs while most mammals give birth to live babies. Tanyka was a "living fossil" in its own time. Because of its weird teeth, scientists think Tanyka munched on plants, which is surprising since its ancient cousins were meat-eaters. We still don't know what its whole body looked like—maybe like a salamander with a long snout, up to three feet long. Finding more bones could solve that mystery and show us how life thrived on the ancient supercontinent Gondwana.
why_it_matters: Learning about ancient 'living fossils' like Tanyka helps us understand where modern animals, including pets and even humans, came from.

keywords.explanation: Something that is bent or turned out of shape, like a pretzel.
A four-legged animal with a backbone, including amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals.
A change in the normal shape of something, often from being squished or damaged.
An animal or plant that has stayed mostly the same for millions of years while its relatives evolved into very different forms.
An ancient supercontinent that once included South America, Africa, Australia, and other landmasses before they drifted apart.
Grew, lived, and did well in a particular place or time.

background_read: Fossils can sometimes look twisted because layers of rock squeeze them over time—but scientists found that this jaw twist was a real feature, not damage.

During the Permian period (around 275 million years ago), most of Earth’s land was joined into a giant supercontinent called Pangaea. It later split into pieces, including Gondwana (an ancient supercontinent made of today’s South America, Africa, Australia, and other continents).
Tetrapods were some of the earliest land animals with backbones, and creatures like Tanyka lived alongside early reptiles and amphibians, showing that ancient ecosystems were full of surprises.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists studying ancient animals, including researcher Jason Pardo.
WHAT: They discovered that Tanyka amnicola had a twisted jaw with sideways teeth and bumpy insides, suggesting it was a plant-eating 'living fossil.'
WHERE: The fossils were dug up from a dry riverbed in Brazil.
WHY: This discovery helps us understand life on the ancient supercontinent Gondwana and shows how some species kept old-fashioned traits while others changed.

perspectives.description: Finding a creature with such a weird jaw is thrilling because it shows how diverse life was long ago.
Without more bones, we can’t be sure about its full body shape, so we should be careful before making big claims.
This discovery helps kids see that science is full of mysteries waiting to be solved.
